items.title: EPID 710: Fundamentals of Epidemiology
items.source_text: An intensive introduction to epidemiological concepts and methods from a perspective of causal inference. The course covers the basics of epidemiologic study designs including randomized trials, observational studies, cohort studies, and case-control studies, along with fundamentals of study conduct and analysis, including concepts such as confounding, effect modification, selection bias, and the use of directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) to identify confounders.

items.title: EPID 718: Analytic Methods in Observational Epidemiology
items.source_text: This course provides an in-depth treatment of the analysis of data from observational epidemiologic studies, including both tabular and regression modeling approaches, with an emphasis on the importance of study design in developing and executing an analysis plan. A major focus of the course is a semester-long, independent data analysis project in which students apply and integrate the concepts covered in class to a dataset and research question of their choosing.

items.title: STOR 565: Machine Learning
items.source_text: Introduction to theory and methods of machine learning including classification; Bayes risk/rule, linear discriminant analysis, logistic regression, nearest neighbors, and support vector machines; clustering algorithms; overfitting, estimation error, cross validation. The course covers a smaller number of representative methods in greater detail, with the goal of illustrating core ideas that underlie many methods, divided into the study of unsupervised methods and supervised methods.

items.title: ECON 416: Behavioral Economics
items.source_text: Four themes of choice are the pillars of the course: choice under certainty (e.g. menu and endowment effects), choice under uncertainty (e.g. prospect theory), intertemporal choice (e.g. hyperbolic preferences), and strategic choices (e.g. social preferences, envy, altruism). First, the course starts with a quick review of the standard economic theory approach to a given topic. Second, data that conflicts with the predictions of the standard model is discussed. Third, alternative behavioral models are introduced.

items.title: PLAN 741: Land Use and Environmental Planning
items.substitution_reason: Direct course page returned catalog listing only; this syllabus PDF is the authoritative public document for this standing course used in subsequent offerings
items.source_text: These sessions introduce fundamental concepts that shape a practitioner's model of land use plan making, including the emerging Smart Growth and Sustainable Development movements; the evolving intergovernmental context for land use and environmental planning; principles of good plans and concepts of plan formats and participatory plan-making processes. The course includes inventory and classification of the environment, suitability analysis for land planning, and the use of GIS and spreadsheets to analyze land suitability and spatial needs.

items.title: EDUC 935: Multilevel Modeling
items.source_text: The primary goal of EDUC 935 is to assist students in learning to think deeply and critically about establishing research problems in social sciences, particularly when the investigation involves data that is meaningfully organized into multiple layers as a hierarchy, or contextual levels. Multilevel models (MLM) are also known as hierarchical linear models (HLM), random coefficient models, or random effects models, and can be used to analyze a variety of questions with either categorical or continuous dependent variables, and explained by many independent variables.
